titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Ernsley Blackwell to Sir Robert Chalmers, 9 June 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Sir Ernsley Blackwell

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Sir Ernley Robertson Hay Blackwell, lawyer and civil servant, was the chief legal advisor to the Cabinet. Blackwell authorised the circulation of Roger Casement's (1864-1916) black diaries during his prosecution. Sir Robert Chalmers (1858 - 1938) was sent to Ireland as replacement under-secretary for Sir Matthew Nathan, who had resigned along with Augustine Birrell, his chief secretary, following the outbreak of the Easter Rising in 1916.

correspAction.placeName: Home Office, Whitehall, London, S.W., England

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
COPY Prison Commission, 9th June, 1916. Sir, I have the honour to report for the information of
the Secretary of State that a question has arisen with
regard to the classification of the Sinn Fein rebels now
located in the English Convict Prisons, many of whom where in
the first instance sentenced to death which was subsequently
commuted to Penal Servitude. The question as to whether
their offence constitutes a felony or misdemeanour is not
clear, and the Directors will be glad to receive the
Secretary of State's instructions in the matter. Two of
the Order of Courts are enclosed (a) where the sentence
first passed was death and (b) where penal servitude was
the original order ..... I am,
Sir,
Your obedient Servant,
(Sd) E. Ruggles Brise
The Under Secretary of State,
&c. &c. &c.
